item.title: A 5733 Tale!
item.content:encoded:
The phenomenal Rounderhouse has written the following tale as his gift for the 2020 Art Exchange. It's a great article that perfectly encapsulates the tone of 5733, so go give it a read!

http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/the-definition-of-madness

One of the few Series VI articles I've come to enjoy! The plot twist was expected, but I also feel like that was the point, wasn't it?

I could imagine the whole thing as I read it, really felt like a trash horror movie! Great stuff, I had a lot of fun.

It's awesome that people are enjoying the article! Just in time for December and the festive season, I present:

A Very Slasher Christmas!

A holiday tale featuring 5733-1 and 5733-2!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Finally an SCP instance that's harmless and won't kill your family in their sleep. I like these more, they're a nice break from the horror articles. It's also why I particularly like Thaumiel class anomalies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that… Well the "When They Cry" horror series certainly cut through my emotions. That's what that reminds me of.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded: "Malcom Pleasance" and "Donald McDowell" had me roaring.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: At first I thought the way the assailant keeps outsmarting the victim was a cop out, but you played off that really well. +1

OMG this is an incredible article with a great end, the development and the addendums are so good, these addendums contribute extremely well to the story of the article. I have nothing more to say, only that this is a wonderful article which has my positive vote.

:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
EMOTION by Carly Rae Jepsen is an underrated piece of pop perfection.

Sequel idea: The Slasher learns to Cut To The Feeling, right through his victims.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once secure in the basement, Heather turns to the camera and says a variation of the following speech:

"Hey, I don't know you, and I don't know why
you've sat there watching, so call me maybe.
But please I beg you, help me out here.
How do I survive? So call me maybe."

I'm sorry, you can ban me.

item.title: Re: Is SCP-5733-1 always Dr Carpenter...
item.content:encoded: Oh… oh no.

item.title: Re: Is SCP-5733-1 always Dr Carpenter...
item.content:encoded: Have another look at Test 017 - should help answer your question on multiple viewers ^_^

item.title: Is SCP-5733-1 always Dr Carpenter...
item.content:encoded:
…or is it whoever is watching?

If it's always Dr Carpenter, what about when there were two instances of it?
If it's the viewer, what about when there are multiple viewers at the same time? Do they each perceive it as themselves?
Or does it only get an identity if the mask is damaged/removed?

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the time I was using Firefox on a [Windows] laptop with 1920 x 1080 resolution.

Embarrassingly, I have to admit that I didn't properly appreciate what the end actually meant on the first read, and having been prodded by you I actually *get it* now — I'm retracting that criticism and replacing it with a much weaker "ending is perhaps not clear enough"!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hi! I’m glad that, on the whole, you enjoyed the article.

I’m sorry you found the CSS annoying, do you mind if I ask what device you use? I’ll bear this in mind for future articles, and try to make them more accessible.

Regarding the ending, it was planned from the start, and I tried to foreshadow the twist in the testing logs: two -1 instances appear when it’s two viewers, when viewed by a thaumaturge the -1 instance knows thaumaturgy. There were a few more obvious indications but these were removed during drafting based on feedback it made it too obvious. Thanks for taking the time to comment, i’ll look to apply your critique to my future drafts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really, really good. Incredible work with the concept and every addendum had a fun ending. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted. Spooky repeating video isn't exactly a new idea, but the choose-your-own-adventure device added some novelty and was effective at drawing me into the story: "What would I tell her to do? What would I do in her position?" Well paced without being stripped to the bone. Somehow I feel like I know the character of Heather despite very little description — part of this is that you're doing a pastiche of something sufficiently cliched that it's pretty easy to fill in the blanks using the surrounding genre tropes, but it's also a testament to the writing, which efficiently and evocatively delivered all those nice little details to interpolate from.

Downsides: I'm afraid to say the CSS was actively annoying! Felt like I was doing foetal heart surgery trying to click those microscopic footnote superscripts, and the collapsible headings went into conniptions when I folded them or happened to drift my cursor over them. I also find the image module which blows up the image on hover pretty pointless and intrusive.

The tests in which the experimenters tell their subjects about the Foundation and magic seemed like such a silly idea in-universe that it broke my suspension of belief. And frankly, they're probably the weakest tests here — part of what hooked me about the premise is imagining myself in that position, you know, scrabbling around in that basement and trying to MacGyver myself out of there with a kid's tricycle and a box of mousetraps, and it's kind of bathetic to go from that headspace to "yeah, just cast expelliarmus and call the Men in Black".

Ending was alright but seemed the story realised it was about to finish and just grabbed at the nearest spooky twist at arm's length. Didn't do much for me. I mean, this would also be an easy way out, but I feel that even just putting another Heather under the hessian would have been cooler and more fitting. Edit: This criticism really should be "ending might not be clear enough" — see below!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice, though I thought it would be more along the lines of you thinking you know what to do in a horror movie and seeing if it's true, probably as a troll by some anartist. Still, I really like it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The answer to all of those questions is: "Who cares?"

Just presume the Foundation did all of that and didn't find anything worth mentioning. An SCP article is flash-fiction — not a 200-page thesis examining the physics of an imaginary magical object.

ETA: Or, more accurately: This SCP article is flash-fiction, not a 200-page thesis. If you want to write an article that prioritizes examining the properties of a fictional object over anything else, you're welcome to do so! This article clearly isn't that, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation missed several opportunities in testing. What happens if its played before an empty room with only a recording device? What happens if its played in an empty room with a camera livestreaming to a test subject in another room? What happens if someone else walks in halfway through the movie? What happens if the viewer walks OUT before the 97 minute mark? Finally (and this one could be rejected by the ethics committee or something) what happens if the viewer self-terminates while watching?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one hits uncomfortably close to home for me.

Whenever i have a bad dream, the recurring theme is being pursued. I always try to either run away, hide from, or somehow trap whoever it is that is chasing me that time, but it never works. Of course the pursuer knows what I am doing, because it's the same brain playing both sides.

I'm sure you can see the parallels here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Crosslink added!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would be honoured to have that crosslink implemented!

Thank you for the feedback. It's really useful, and hopefully as I develop as an author I can work on those points in future articles. The alternative idea to the cul-de-sac loop is fantastic. Glad you enjoyed the article, and thank you for commenting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a lot of fun. Given the burlap sack over-the-head and whole slasher-film aesthetic, I'm tempted (with your permission) to go back and edit Straight to VHS: Sunday Dinner to have a reference to Crystal Elms Productions (including a crosslink).

FEEDBACK
– hide block

There are aspects where I think this could be a lot cleaner and tighter, but most of my issues are quibbles: I feel like you could cut the strikethrough part of the containment procedures out entirely, the description could be consistently tighter (you don't need to tell us that the murders occur 'in a manner consistent with contemporary horror tropes'), and I suspect there's a less 'immersion-breaking' way to ease us into the anomaly's primary mechanic (providing advice to Heather). Having her literally turn to the camera and plead for help feels… a bit clumsy. At the point in the basement, she could just suddenly become capable of HEARING the audience.

I'd also cut out the bit where she ignores conversations not relevant to the monster — you don't need to tell us that (and honestly, it'd be even more interesting to replace it with some vague reference to 'interviews' researchers have conducted with her).

I also feel like the ending where she loops back into the town was predictable — I would have preferred something more vague and creepy, like how SCP-2614 ends. She comes across a town, but the town is empty; the whole WORLD is empty. Nothing exists save for her and the killer stalking her, now — etc. I feel like this — rather than the cyclical 'loops back around to the cul-de-sac' — drives home the sense of despair and inevitability of her fate. It's not that she's trapped here — she can go wherever she wants! It's just that the world she exists in is one in which her only purpose is to be brutally murdered for our viewing entertainment. One can imagine her running into a police station to find no one there; the guns are all just props, or chunks of plastic made to look like guns. The farther she goes out, the more 'artificial' the world becomes — etc. Something like that.

Again, though — these are all quibbles. The core concept here is fun; the test-logs are great, and remind me a lot of the 2007 Funny Games (it's a remake of an 1997 Austrian film of the same name, but I never saw the original). Deeply sadistic in a fourth-wall breaking way that re-imagines the slasher film as an existential horror about the inescapability of dying violently when you're in a horror film.

Basically, easy upvote — I think there's lots of places this could be much tighter, but all in all, it's a pretty good execution of a great idea.

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Frankly, the fact that his first name is Dario really gets me.

Alice Strode and "Crystal Elms Productions" got a very hearty laugh out of me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This makes me wonder what would happen if this tape were played near SCP-3922…

item.title: The Holy Grail of SCPs
item.content:encoded: This whole article is an outstanding piece of work. Amazing description and storytelling techniques, this is by far one of the best SCPs on this entire site in my opinion… and that ending! You couldn't have executed it more perfectly! You should feel very proud of yourself, because you have done something many people can only dream of being able to accomplish! I look forward to reading any more of your future works!
And just in time for Friday the 13th!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So many horror movie references. Love it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A really excellent article; reminds me strongly of second series classics. I was surprised by how much I felt for Heather and Dr. Carpenter at the end: Carpenter was so close to 'saving her', if only he'd used a neutral subject, but now they're going to shelve testing so he can't do it properly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Given the revelation at the end of the final log, I'm surprised the Foundation didn't try playing the tape to an empty room afterwards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually don't watch a ton of horror movies, so the references didn't really mess up my immersion into the story. I'm giving it a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(Plus, I'm not sure how this hasn't been pointed out yet, but both the collapsibles still have XXXX on them.)

This is a problem with the collapsible component that's being used, not with the article itself. In the mean time until it gets fixed, the following can patch it

 [[module CSS]] #page-content .collapsible-block-folded .collapsible-block-link::before { content: "▷ ADDENDUM 5733." counter(collapse); } #page-content .collapsible-block-unfolded .collapsible-block-link::before { content: "▽ ADDENDUM 5733." counter(collapse); } [[/module]]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel I need to further iterate on this; my problem isn't necessarily that they're references, it's that they're kind of shallow ones. You miss an opportunity to have the Pleasance-McDowell test be the one that features Mr. Loomis (since both Malcolm McDowell and Donald Pleasance both played different iterations of that character), or have Baker tell Alice to try to go for a decapitation (which is how the final kill in the original Sleepaway Camp was delivered) or have Englund-Daskewisz advise setting the killer on fire (as both Freddy and Jason have died by fire at least once). The closest you come to this is Bennett the thaumaturgist, who (I admittedly had to look this up) is named for Joan Bennett, an actress in Dario Argento's Suprisia, which has occult elements. Carpenter is also a good example, with the meta-horror twist recalling Mouth of Madness.

Honestly, this is one of those SCPs that seriously needs an annotated version, one that explains various in-jokes and references to you. I'm still not sure about upvoting it, considering the above issues.

(Plus, I'm not sure how this hasn't been pointed out yet, but both the collapsibles still have XXXX on them.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This will be getting my downvote as of now, and I'll be explaining my reasoning below as to why it got my downvote.

The Main Reasons
...
Reason 1: You never clarified explicitly what happens should you communicate with SCP-5733-2

Referring to this paragraph in question.

Following this, the viewer of SCP-5733 can directly converse with Heather, henceforth designated SCP-5733-2, and advise her on how to escape from SCP-5733-1. The course of the movie then depends on the conversations held with SCP-5733-2. SCP-5733-2 will only converse with the viewer on the aforementioned topic. If the viewer ignores SCP-5733-2 or attempts to talk to her concerning topics other than SCP-5733-15, she, in a resigned manner, will walk back up the basement stairs, unlock and open the door. SCP-5733-1 will be waiting directly outside the door. The film will then cut to black and SCP-5733 ejects itself from the machine on which it is being played.

Okay, this ones kind of a no-brainer, but I still feel it applies. The logs may occur immediately after this initial point is mentioned, but this is meant to be a formal document. There should be a part explaining what happens should you choose to communicate with SCP-5733-2. This may be a rather nit-picky thing on my end, but since it's never clarified directly, I feel like it's glossed over. There should be a part in which it explains how the course of action occurs should you communicate with SCP-5733-2, mentioning how SCP-5733-1 appears to be aware of these things, and adjust accordingly.

Reason 2: The logs drag out for me

This one applies in a strange way, but it still deserves to be mentioned. The amount of logs here are quite high. The one about suicide is especially an offender of this, as being advised to kill yourself isn't advice you typically would take up. This point resonates for me pretty strongly.

Reason 3: The end is just to expected

This specific point may be somewhat controversial, but in my eyes, the ending was just sort of… there? It didn't feel special, and it felt like something I expected the moment I heard of the logs. Typically, this wouldn't stand with me as well, however, in this case, it feels lazy. This may be due to the trope, and if thats the case feel free to tell me (I'm not one to watch movies), but this point just sticks out to me.

Because of these reasons, I will be firm on my downvote of this article, as I am not one to leave no-votes on any article.

snipped

Edit: After re-reading the article more intently, I have decided to add a fourth reason the list of reasons why I downvoted, which helps me be more firm in my downvote.

EDIT:4th reason
...
Reason 4: The motto

This is a controversial take, which is why I was hesitant on adding it to the list as a given. However, for the sake of feeling better about my downvote, I have hesitantly chosen to include it here.

Why would the foundation feel the need to do this much testing?

I have always been a firm believer in the idea that the foundation does what its namesake tells it to. They secure, they contain, and they protect. This SCP can be stored within a standard containment unit, and then no need to conduct further testing would be necessary. I always feel there should be a strong reason as to why the foundation chooses to test on a specific SCP. In this case, it feels like there is no reason to conduct further testing on the SCP itself, when it's easy to contain as is. The Entity within the film has no reason to be suspected of being the researcher themself (even if its an expected viewpoint out-of-universe). The test logs feel like they should have a reason to exist, or else its testing for the sake of testing, which is what they want to avoid. Sure, testing may help them contain the anomaly better, but in this case, it's a VHS which can be stored away.

This point may not stand well to some, but for me, this is a strong indicator of the why not syndrome, which is why I chose to leave a downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is exactly what I was going for! How we're simultaneously rooting for both the protagonist and antagonist of those movies simultaneously. Thanks for the typo catch, it's been corrected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The tests could be cleaned up a bit to drag on a bit less (especially the build up in the last addendum), but the ending makes up for any small issues.

The increased abilities of the killer and other plot armor suddenly make sense with the final reveal — and it's almost a commentary on slasher films? We as the viewer, we kind of want the victims to die, after all why else are we watching the movie?

Cool stuff.
Never thought an article of mine would hit 50 in 24 hours, but here we are! Really pleased that people like it, I'll try not to let it be 2 years until my next article…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was beautifully done. I don't care for no-win situations (Koboyashu Maru), but you set everything up for a satisfying conclusion.

The long nighttime walk through dark nothingness back to the murder scene was especially disturbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved it in crit, love it now. Ending was a great turn-around, and the methods of elaborate murders were so on-point for 80s slashers. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't have an answer for the teaching magic point, but I don't think they gave it a working hotline number. The way the sentence is worded implies that the number was working in 1983 but not when the test was being conducted, which is some years later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a very fair critique - the article is intended to be a tribute to those movies, but I can understand that the names are too much for some readers. I've updated the particular name you reference. The reason it was a straight copy was due to Sleepaway Camp being less well known than the other movies referenced. Thanks for commenting and pointing out that this was something I could improve!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No-voting because literally every named character in this being a horror movie shout-out is distracting to the point where it pulls me out of the story. The first few references were cute, but after you literally named a character after the main actress in Sleepaway Camp, I couldn't help but roll my eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great SCP loved it from back when it was a draft you really did great on it even if i kinda predicted how it works

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I expected the ending but that doesn't really matter because it was just aa fun as the journey there. The test logs are engaging, fun, paced well, just enjoyable to read. It definitely reads like a scippification of 80s slasher moves, kinda like Too Many Cooks — great stuff, loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't think of a good reason for the Foundation to OK tests where they give an anomaly the number of a working Foundation hotline or teach it magic, but I'm willing to let that pass because the test logs are still engaging, entertaining, and don't overstay their welcome. The ending is also superlative.

I gotta say, this is definitely one of my favorites on this site. I've always been a sucker for 'big reveal' horror, and this is no exception.

Hard upvote, 10/10 would read again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The ending absolutely blew my mind. Holy shit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great. Wonderful job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved it during crit, and still love it now!

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't particularly have a fondness for slasher films, but I appreciate the genre, and I feel the same way about this SCP. While the story nor even the core anomaly didn't play to my particular tastes on what I like to read on the wiki, this was very well done, and it was obvious a lot of time, effort and live went into this. Besides, it was just fun finding all of the easter eggs and references.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is genuinely the best thing I’ve read all month. Any minor issues I encountered while reading are easily forgivable due to the creative execution the article takes. The ending could’ve been seen from a mile away, but, like these cheesy horror movies, it’s about the journey there, not the destination.

My only complaint is that the ending log cribs a little too much from SCP-2614 for my liking. If it cut straight to returning to the cul-de-sac, it wouldn’t break pacing as much.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, this beats whatever the fuck I was going to do with Corpse Club by a mile. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. Reminds me of those books where you'd choose where to go, but in a more interactive form. +1

item.title: Re: +1
item.content:encoded:
Fun Fact: every name within the article is a reference to one or more classic horror movies!

Glad you enjoyed it! If there's room for 12 films in the Friday the 13th franchise, there's definitely room for multiple Halloween slasher skips!

… Dr. Carpenter. Of course.

I've also been meaning to do a Halloween slasher SCP, but this one is great!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the way the killer acts, scary in the moment but kind of funny as an observer. Good stuff.

item.title: SCP-5733: Knife. Scream. Cut to Black.
item.content:encoded:
Inspired by a love of '80s slasher movies and horror movies in general. This idea first came to me a few days prior to Halloween in the midst of a marathon of the aforementioned movie genres.

This is my first SCP in over 2 years, a massive thank you goes to all those who offered critique and guidance: TheShutteredEye, Proasek, Phiiota, RadioactiveRADS, Elunerazim, and Snapdragon133.

+ Spoilers
- Spoilers

SCP-5733-1 takes on the identity of whoever is watching SCP-5733. It's why -1 is always one step ahead, why it knows thaumaturgy in test 28, and why there are two instances in test 017.

+ Image Credits
- Image Credits

Image is a composite of the following:
https://pixy.org/5717529/
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Handmade_chef_knife.jpg
https://www.deviantart.com/fearoftheblackwolf/art/VHS-Box-Texture-534099807
https://www.font space.com/bacon-font-f7372
